text:
# Section Framework Reference

## Section Order (Default)

1. Name & Contact (always first)
2. Professional Summary
3. Skills
4. Experience
5. Projects (if included)
6. Education
7. Certifications (if included)

This order works for 90% of cases. Exceptions: freshers with no experience should lead with Education, then Projects, then Internships. Career switchers may lead with a custom "Relevant Experience" section.

## Section 1: Name & Contact

### Format
```
Name - Target Role Title
Location | Email | Phone | LinkedIn | GitHub | Portfolio
```

### Rules
- Name and title should be the same font size, both bold
- Contact info on one line, centered, smaller font
- Email and LinkedIn MUST be clickable hyperlinks
- GitHub link only if the profile has meaningful activity (not an empty account)
- Portfolio/website link only if it's live, maintained, and reinforces positioning
- No physical address beyond city/country
- No photo (unless required by regional norms)

### Common Mistakes
- Using "Curriculum Vitae" or "Resume" as a header (unnecessary, everyone knows what this document is)
- Including date of birth, marital status, or nationality (not needed in most markets)
- Listing a personal website that's under construction or broken
- Using a non-professional email address

## Section 2: Professional Summary

### Purpose
Give the hiring manager the complete picture in 3-5 sentences. If they read nothing else, they should know: what you do, what makes you special, and the scale of your impact.

### Framework
Sentence 1: [Role] with [X years] of experience [core competency].
Sentence 2: [Biggest achievement with specific metrics].
Sentence 3: [Second biggest achievement or scope indicator].
Sentence 4: [Credibility signal — promotions, notable clients, team leadership].
Sentence 5: [Technical skills if relevant to the role — "Hands-on with X, Y, Z"].

### Rules
- Every sentence must contain a provable fact. No opinions, no adjectives.
- Bold the key numbers: years of experience, user counts, customer counts, team size.
- Do NOT include: "passionate about," "results-oriented," "team player," or any other filler.
- The summary should be readable in under 10 seconds.
- If the user's summary is longer than 5 sentences, it's too long.

### Review Checklist
- [ ] Does it answer "what does this person do?" in sentence 1?
- [ ] Does it include at least one quantified achievement?
- [ ] Is there a credibility signal (promotion, notable client, team size)?
- [ ] Could this summary belong to 100 other people? (If yes, it's too generic)
- [ ] Are there any adjectives that aren't backed by evidence?

### Fresher Variation
Freshers don't have professional achievements. Lead with education, technical skills, and academic/personal projects:
"Computer Science graduate from [University] with [GPA/distinction]. Built [project] that [outcome]. Technical skills in [X, Y, Z]."

## Section 3: Skills

### Purpose
Quick scan for hiring managers and ATS keyword matching. This is NOT the place to demonstrate expertise — that's what Experience is for.

### Framework
Two categories maximum:
- **Technical:** Languages, frameworks, tools, platforms actually used
- **Product/Domain:** Methodologies, practices relevant to the target role

Optional third line:
- **Also worked with:** Lower-priority tools for ATS coverage

### Rules
- Only list skills you can discuss intelligently in an interview
- AI-assisted usage counts (e.g., using Next.js with Claude Code is still "Next.js" experience)
- Never create subcategories like "Beginner/Intermediate/Advanced" — they invite doubt
- Remove any skill that doesn't appear in job descriptions for your target role
- Technical skills you actually use daily should come first in the list
- Kill buzzword frameworks: RACI Matrix, Moscow Method, Product Vision Board, Design Thinking as standalone skills. These are methodologies that belong in context, not in a keyword list.

### ATS Consideration
If keywords are removed from Skills, they must appear naturally in Experience bullets. ATS scans the entire document, not just the skills section. The resume's total keyword coverage matters, not where keywords appear.

### Fresher Variation
Freshers can be more generous with skills since their Experience section is thin. Include academic tools, lab equipment, languages, and relevant coursework topics. But still avoid listing things you can't discuss.

## Section 4: Experience

### Purpose
The core of the resume. This is where hiring decisions are made or lost.

### Structure Options

**Option A: Single consolidated entry per company (recommended for most)**
```
Company Name                                          Location
Current Title                                    Start Date - Present
Promoted: Previous Title → Current Title
• Achievement bullet 1
• Achievement bullet 2
...
```

**Option B: Separate entries per role (use when roles were fundamentally different)**
```
Company Name                                          Location

Senior Engineer                                  2022 - Present
• Achievement bullets for this role

Junior Engineer                                  2020 - 2022
• Achievement bullets for this role
```

Use Option A when: Same company, same general function, promotions show progression but the work is a continuous story.
Use Option B when: Roles were genuinely different (e.g., moved from engineering to product within the same company).

### The Perfect Bullet Formula
```
[Action verb] + [what you built/did] + [scale/scope] + [measurable outcome]
```

Examples:
- "Built an internal vendor intelligence platform replacing manual workflows across 23 countries and 1,500+ vendors. Reduced evaluation time from 1-3 days to under 10 minutes."
- "Led a 4-person engineering team delivering 4 AI POCs for an MBB consulting firm in collaboration with a major cloud provider."
- "Redesigned the checkout flow for a B2C e-commerce platform, increasing conversion rate from 2.1% to 3.8%."

### Metric Extraction Questions
When the user doesn't have metrics, probe with:
- "What was the process BEFORE you did this? How long did it take?"
- "How many people use what you built?"
- "How many [units of work] did you process/handle?"
- "What was the team size you worked with?"
- "Did this replace a manual process? What did that cost?"
- "Can you compare before vs after in any measurable way?"

If probing yields nothing concrete, write the bullet without a metric rather than fabricating one. A honest "Built X for Y team" is better than a fake "Improved Z by 40%."

### Bold Strategy
Bold key metrics and achievement numbers to create visual anchors for scanning:
- User/customer counts: **20+ enterprise customers**, **1,000+ users**
- Time improvements: **1-2 weeks to under a day**
- Scale indicators: **23 countries**, **1,500+ vendors**
- Notable collaborations: **4 AI POCs for an MBB consulting firm**

Do NOT bold everything. If more than 30% of text is bold, nothing stands out.

### Link Strategy
Make product names clickable when they have live URLs:
- Company products the user built (if public)
- Open source projects with GitHub repos
- Published apps or websites
- Portfolio pieces

Do NOT link to: internal tools (obvious), broken/dead URLs, private repos.

### Chronology
Most recent role first, always. Within a role, most impactful bullet first, not chronological order of when things happened.

### Handling NDAs
When the user can't name clients or projects:
- Use category descriptors: "MBB consulting firm," "Fortune 500 retail client," "Big 4 bank"
- Never name the specific company
- Category should be specific enough to signal caliber but vague enough to protect identity
- "A top-tier consulting firm" is weaker than "an MBB consulting firm" (3 companies vs hundreds)

### What to Cut
- Bullets that describe duties rather than achievements ("Responsible for managing...")
- Bullets that repeat achievements from other sections
- Metrics that conflict with other metrics on the same resume
- Anything the user can't confidently explain in an interview
- Old roles that add no value to the current narrative (usually 8+ years old)

## Section 5: Projects

### When to Include
- Freshers: ALWAYS. This is your Experience substitute.
- Early career: Only if projects reinforce your positioning AND are shipped/live.
- Mid-career+: Only exceptional projects (open source with stars, products with real users).

### Rules
- Only include projects that are shipped and accessible. "Built but never launched" is not a project; it's a learning exercise.
- Include the tech stack for each project.
- Include a clickable link (GitHub, live URL) when available.
- If the project is dead/offline, don't include it.

### Fresher Project Framework
```
Project Name (link)                              Date Range
• One-line description of what it does and who uses it
• Tech stack: X, Y, Z
• Key metric or outcome (downloads, users, stars, grade received)
```

## Section 6: Education

### Format by Career Level

**Fresher:**
```
University Name                                   Location
Degree, Specialization - GPA/CGPA               Year Range
Relevant coursework: X, Y, Z (only if directly relevant to target role)

School Name                                       Location  
Class XII, Board - Percentage                     Year
Class X, Board - Percentage                       Year
```

**Early Career (1-4 years):**
```
University Name                                   Location
Degree, Specialization - GPA/CGPA               Year Range

School Name                                       Location
Class XII, Board - Percentage                     Year
Class X, Board - Percentage                       Year
```

**Mid-Career (5+ years):**
```
University Name                                   Location
Degree, Specialization                           Year Range
```
No school marks. No coursework. One line.

### Rules
- GPA only if above 7.5/10 or 3.5/4.0. Below that, omit it.
- School marks: Include for Indian resumes under 5 years experience (application portals parse them). Compress to single lines.
- No school names needed for 5+ years experience.
- Certifications go in a separate section, not under Education.

## Section 7: Certifications (Optional)

### When to Include
- The certification is recognized in the industry (AWS, GCP, PMP, CFA)
- It's relevant to the target role
- It was earned recently (within 3 years)

### When to Exclude
- Udemy, Coursera, or similar online course completions (these are not certifications)
- Expired certifications
- Certifications irrelevant to the target role
- If the resume is already at one strong page, certifications can justify a clean second page alongside other content

## Internships (for early career)

### Rules
- Keep for anyone under 3 years of professional experience
- Compress to 1-2 bullets maximum per internship
- Remove for anyone over 5 years of experience
- Only keep internships at recognizable companies or with relevant work
- If the internship work is similar to current work, it's redundant and should be cut

## General Formatting Rules

### Page Count
- Always TARGET one page first. Tighten spacing, compress weak sections, and cut low-value content before expanding.
- If after honest optimization the content is genuinely strong and doesn't fit, expand to two pages. Every line on page two must make the hiring manager more interested, not fill space.
- Two pages of strong content beats one page with critical achievements cut.
- Three pages maximum for 15+ years of experience.
- Academic CVs: As many pages as needed.

### Font
- Arial or Calibri throughout.
- Name: 16pt bold.
- Section headings: 11pt bold, uppercase, with bottom border.
- Body text: 10pt.
- Contact info: 9pt.

### Spacing
- Tight but readable. No double spacing anywhere.
- Consistent spacing between sections.
- Bullet points with minimal spacing (30-40 DXA before/after).

### Alignment
- All dates and locations right-aligned using tab stops.
- Content width = page width minus margins.
- Tab stop position must match content width exactly for clean right-alignment.

### Bullet Points
- Use proper bullet formatting (LevelFormat.BULLET in docx-js), never unicode characters.
- Each bullet should be 1-3 lines. Anything longer needs to be split or cut.
- Start every bullet with a strong action verb: Built, Led, Designed, Delivered, Reduced, Increased, Launched, Architected, Managed, Shipped.
